en.label: Social Media
en.why: Endless scroll, comparison, and algorithm-driven content impact sleep, focus, and self-esteem.
en.opening: "I've been worried about screen time — I want to understand what pulls you to Instagram â can we talk for a minute, not to get you in trouble, just so I understand what you like about it?"

pitfalls: Don't demand phone access at random â it breaks trust fast.
Don't moralize ("back in my dayâŚ") â they tune out within 10 seconds.
Don't focus on screen time numbers. Focus on how the time FEELS to them.

ageNotes.note: Most platforms have a 13+ age rule for a reason. Frame as "that app is for older kids" not "you're too immature".
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: This is when social comparison hits hardest. Ask what posts make them feel good vs. drained.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Acknowledge it's how their friend group communicates. Negotiate boundaries instead of banning.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Acknowledge it's how their friend group communicates. Negotiate boundaries instead of banning.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "Everyone else has it"
reactions.example: They insist all their classmates use the app and they'll be left out.
reactions.parentResponse: "I believe you that it feels that way. Let's talk to two friends' parents together to see what they actually allow â then we decide."
reactions.avoid: Calling them a liar or saying "I don't care what everyone else does".

reactions.label: Silence / shrug
reactions.example: They go quiet or give one-word answers.
reactions.parentResponse: "That's fine, we don't have to solve it now. Think about it and tell me tomorrow what would feel fair to you."
reactions.avoid: Filling the silence with your own monologue.

reactions.label: Anger / slammed door
reactions.example: "You're controlling me! You don't trust me!"
reactions.parentResponse: "I hear that you're upset. I'm not trying to control you â I want to keep you safe. Let's pause and come back when we're both calmer."
reactions.avoid: Matching their volume. Escalation ends the conversation.

reactions.label: Tears / shame
reactions.example: They cry or hide their face.
reactions.parentResponse: "You're not in trouble. I'm on your side. Take a breath â we'll figure this out together."
reactions.avoid: Rushing to "fix" the feeling with logic.

reactions.label: Negotiation
reactions.example: "What if I only use it for an hour, only on weekends?"
reactions.parentResponse: "That's a reasonable start. Let's try it for two weeks, then we both check in â you tell me how it felt and we adjust."
reactions.avoid: Agreeing then silently tightening the rules later.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: keep it short (max 5 minutes), then pause. ASD: announce the conversation a day in advance, avoid metaphors. Generalised anxiety: name the emotion out loud — 'I can see this is stressing you.'

redFlags.flag: Losing sleep to scroll past midnight more than 3 nights a week.
redFlags.action: Agree together on a phone cut-off time for the night.

redFlags.flag: Mood crashes within minutes of closing the app.
redFlags.action: Talk about which specific accounts trigger the mood drop — mute or unfollow them.

redFlags.flag: Secrecy with specific accounts (hidden, "finstas", locked messages from strangers).
redFlags.action: Private chats with strangers are a red alert — require immediate conversation.

followUp: Write down the agreed rule together on paper â not just verbal.
Schedule a 2-week check-in on the calendar, visible to both.
Install one shared app (e.g. a game) you can both play â rebuilds trust.
en.repairAfterConflict: If the door slammed — within 24h go to them alone: 'I didn't handle this well earlier. I'm sorry I raised my voice. I still want to talk when you're ready.' Do not re-open the topic in that repair conversation.
en.positiveReinforcement: When your child shows you a funny reel or tells you about a creator on their own — that's GOLD. Instead of judging, say: 'I'm glad you shared that, tell me more.'
en.culturalContext: In many cultures the dominant style is 'don't talk back to parents' — it closes conversations. Try 'I might be wrong, tell me where' — giving away some control builds trust.

en.label: Adult Content
en.why: Early exposure shapes ideas about consent, bodies, and relationships â often in harmful ways.
en.opening: "I want to talk about something that might feel awkward â it's okay to be embarrassed, I'll be too. What's important is that you know you're not in trouble."
en.parentSelfRegulation: Your physical reaction (blushing, tension) reaches the child before your words. Rehearse in a mirror. If you are burning with shame — wait a day.
pitfalls: Don't ask "did you watch it?" â yes/no interrogations shut them down.
Don't pretend it doesn't exist. They know it does.
Don't weaponize shame. Shame drives the behavior underground.

ageNotes.note: Explain: "Some websites show grown-up things that aren't meant for you â tell me if it pops up, you won't be in trouble."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Curiosity is normal. Separate "curious about bodies" (normal) from "watching porn" (distorts what real intimacy is).
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Talk about porn vs. real relationships: consent, stopping, communication â none of that shows in most porn.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Talk about porn vs. real relationships: consent, stopping, communication â none of that shows in most porn.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Deflection / joking
reactions.example: They laugh it off or make it a joke.
reactions.parentResponse: "I know this is awkward. I'll be brief â I just want you to know you can ask me anything without being judged."
reactions.avoid: Getting annoyed that they're not "being serious".

reactions.label: Embarrassed silence
reactions.example: Bright red face, looks at the floor.
reactions.parentResponse: "You don't have to say anything. Just listen. If you ever have a question, I promise I won't make it weird."
reactions.avoid: Forcing eye contact.

reactions.label: Defensive anger
reactions.example: "That's disgusting, I would never!"
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay. I'm not accusing you â I'm telling you that if it ever happens, you won't lose my trust by telling me."
reactions.avoid: Arguing about whether they did or didn't.

reactions.label: Honest admission
reactions.example: "Yeah, a friend showed me something."
reactions.parentResponse: "Thanks for telling me. How did it make you feel? That feeling matters more than the content."
reactions.avoid: Punishing the honesty â you'll never hear the truth again.

reactions.label: Questions
reactions.example: "Why do people watch that?"
reactions.parentResponse: "Good question. Curiosity is normal. The problem is most of it shows a fake version of intimacy â no respect, no consent. Let's talk about what real relationships look like."
reactions.avoid: Dodging the question.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: prepare one key sentence and stick to it. ASD: use clear anatomical names instead of metaphors ('pornography' — not 'that kind of stuff'). Quiet setting, minimal stimulation.

redFlags.flag: Compulsive use (hours per day, affecting school/sleep).
redFlags.action: Several hours a day crosses into compulsion — specialist needed.

redFlags.flag: Describing violent or non-consensual scenarios as "normal".
redFlags.action: Describing violent scenes as "normal" calls for psychological consultation.

redFlags.flag: Any mention of an adult initiating sexual conversation online â call authorities immediately.
redFlags.action: Any adult initiating sexual conversation — call police and CyberTipline immediately.

followUp: Put devices in shared spaces (not bedrooms) for a while, framed as "for all of us".
Book a session with a family therapist if the topic keeps coming up â they're trained for this.
Leave the door open: "You can come back to this conversation anytime."
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child shut down or ran off — DO NOT chase them immediately. Leave a note: 'I'm here when you're ready. Nothing you said changes that I love you.' Come back in 2–3 hours.
en.positiveReinforcement: If the child asks you anything about sexuality on their own — that's a trophy. Say: 'I'm glad you asked me, not Google.' Even if the question surprised you.
en.culturalContext: In many countries school sex-education is thin, and pornography becomes the first source. Your conversation MUST replace that. Resources: Planned Parenthood (US), NHS (UK), Scarleteen.

en.label: Gambling
en.why: Gambling sites (and loot boxes in games) exploit the same dopamine circuits as cocaine â the teenage brain is extra vulnerable.
en.opening: "I saw a betting site got blocked. I'm not mad â I want to understand. Was it something a friend sent you, or were you just curious?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: If you play the lottery — notice your child is watching. Before the talk: 'Could my own gambling be modelling this?' Honesty builds authority.
pitfalls: Don't confuse "skill" with "gambling" â poker with friends is different from online casino.
Don't ignore loot boxes. They train the same pattern.
Don't moralize about money. Focus on how gambling is engineered to feel good even when you lose.

ageNotes.note: Frame as: "These are designed to trick adults into losing money â your brain would have no chance."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Explain house edge: "The site always wins long-term. It's math, not luck."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Talk about crypto/sports betting hype on TikTok â influencers get paid to lose publicly.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Talk about crypto/sports betting hype on TikTok â influencers get paid to lose publicly.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "I was just looking"
reactions.example: They insist they never placed a bet.
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay. I believe curiosity is the most common reason. Let me show you how these sites are designed â it's kind of interesting."
reactions.avoid: Accusations without evidence.

reactions.label: "It's my money"
reactions.example: They argue about allowance or earned money.
reactions.parentResponse: "You're right, it's yours. My job is to make sure you don't lose it to a site that's legally off-limits until you're 18."
reactions.avoid: Taking the money away as punishment.

reactions.label: Bragging about winning
reactions.example: "I turned âŹ5 into âŹ40!"
reactions.parentResponse: "Cool win. Now let's track every session for a week â wins and losses â on paper. You'll see what the real number is."
reactions.avoid: Mocking or dismissing the win. They'll hide the losses.

reactions.label: Hiding losses
reactions.example: You find they've lost significant money.
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm proud you didn't lie when I asked. Losing money is scary â let's figure out a plan, not a punishment."
reactions.avoid: Bailing them out without a conversation about the habit.

reactions.label: Denial after obvious use
reactions.example: Browser history + blocked attempts exist, they deny.
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm going to show you what I saw, not to corner you, but because I'd rather talk than pretend."
reactions.avoid: Leading them into a lie before revealing you know.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: higher addiction risk via dopamine loops (loot boxes, betting). Preventive conversation BEFORE a problem. ASD: teach mathematically — house edge, expected value.

redFlags.flag: Borrowing money from friends or siblings repeatedly.
redFlags.action: Borrowing from peers is a classic addiction pattern — conversation + monitoring.

redFlags.flag: Selling possessions (games, skins) to fund accounts.
redFlags.action: Selling belongings to fund gambling accounts — time for an addiction specialist.

redFlags.flag: Secrecy about payment methods / using parents' cards without asking.
redFlags.action: Hidden use of parents' cards is theft but also a compulsion signal.

followUp: Install Gamban or similar blocker (not just DNS-level).
Help them set a "cooling-off" period (30 days) and reward milestones.
Share GamCare (UK), Anonimowi HazardziĹci (PL), or local teen-focused support.
en.repairAfterConflict: After a money fight: short SMS — 'Call me when you're ready. I won't back down from helping, but I'll talk without yelling.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child admits a small loss — gold. Don't punish. 'Thank you for telling me. That means you trust me. We'll sort it out together.'
en.culturalContext: Gambling is normalised via ads. Watch one ad together, count who wins long-term. Resources: GamCare (UK), NCPG (US 1-800-522-4700).

en.label: Drugs
en.why: Kids encounter drug content (normalization, sales, "research chemicals") long before offline exposure. Conversations must come first.
en.opening: "I saw a site got blocked that was about drugs. I'd rather talk to you than assume â what were you trying to find out?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: If you drank or smoked in your youth — decide before the talk how much you'll share. A lie ('never!') kills credibility; full honesty may be too much. Choose the middle.
pitfalls: Don't use scare tactics from the 90s â kids have Google, they'll fact-check you.
Don't conflate weed, MDMA, and heroin â credibility dies if you do.
Don't punish curiosity. Punish dishonesty.

ageNotes.note: Focus on medicines: "Only take what we or a doctor gives you. Never try something a friend offers."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Talk about vaping and energy drinks as gateway habits (real, not moral panic).
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Be honest about risk differences. "Weed isn't heroin. But a developing brain reacts differently to everything."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Be honest about risk differences. "Weed isn't heroin. But a developing brain reacts differently to everything."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "I was just curious"
reactions.example: They claim research, not use.
reactions.parentResponse: "Fair. What did you want to know? Let's look it up on Erowid or a harm-reduction site together â not a dealer's page."
reactions.avoid: Assuming "curious" = "using".

reactions.label: "A friend does it"
reactions.example: They deflect to someone else.
reactions.parentResponse: "Thanks for telling me. I'm not going to rat them out. I want to know: are you worried about them?"
reactions.avoid: Banning the friend without a conversation.

reactions.label: Admitting use
reactions.example: "I tried weed once."
reactions.parentResponse: "I appreciate you telling me. That took courage. How was it? What would you want to do differently?"
reactions.avoid: Immediate punishment â the next honest conversation won't happen.

reactions.label: Anger / walkout
reactions.example: "You're accusing me of being a junkie!"
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm not. Go cool off. When you come back I'd like to hear what made that feel like an accusation."
reactions.avoid: Following them yelling.

reactions.label: Joking / minimizing
reactions.example: "Dad, everyone drinks, chill."
reactions.parentResponse: "Drinking isn't the same as trying research chemicals you bought online. Those are cooked in labs with no quality control."
reactions.avoid: Matching the joke and dropping the subject.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: 2–4x higher addiction risk — preventive conversation earlier than for neurotypical peers. ASD: holds tightly to rules — base it on numbers and data.

redFlags.flag: Unexplained cash or missing money.
redFlags.action: A conversation and pocket-money budgeting.

redFlags.flag: New friend group + secrecy + mood swings combined.
redFlags.action: Warning constellation — conversation + school contact.

redFlags.flag: Paraphernalia found in room or car.
redFlags.action: Drug paraphernalia at home — family doctor consultation.

redFlags.flag: Any mention of opioids, benzos, or unknown pills â immediate doctor visit.
redFlags.action: Opioids, benzodiazepines, or unknown pills — straight to hospital.

followUp: Keep medicines locked. Count pills if needed.
Know the signs of overdose for the region's common substances â have Narcan/naloxone if relevant.
Refer to adolescent-specialist counselor, not a general therapist.
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child ran out — don't call them for hours. 'All I want is for you to come back safely. The rest can wait.' Reassure no punishment awaits.
en.positiveReinforcement: When a child REFUSES drugs in a peer group — an adulthood exam. Praise openly (with siblings, grandparents).
en.culturalContext: Designer drugs still sold via Telegram. Resources: SAMHSA helpline 1-800-662-HELP (US), FRANK 0300 123 6600 (UK). Find a local adolescent specialist.

en.label: Self-harm / Pro-ana
en.why: Pro-ana and self-harm communities actively teach kids to hide symptoms and compete on severity. Attempted access is urgent.
en.opening: "I love you. I'm not here to punish you â I'm here because I'm worried. Can you tell me how you've been feeling lately?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: THIS is the conversation where you must be fully calm. If you have your own history of mood disorders — call the other parent, a friend, a therapist BEFORE the talk.
pitfalls: Don't ask "are you cutting yourself?" as a yes/no â invites a lie.
Don't promise secrecy you can't keep. Say: "I will always tell your other parent / doctor if it's serious. Always."
Don't leave them alone after a tense disclosure for at least a few hours.

ageNotes.note: Keep language simple: "Some websites teach kids to hurt themselves. That's never a way to feel better â let's figure out what is."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Body-image attacks peak here. Talk about filters and how NOBODY looks like Instagram photos.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Be direct: "If you're thinking about hurting yourself or not eating, I'd rather hear it from you than find out later."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Be direct: "If you're thinking about hurting yourself or not eating, I'd rather hear it from you than find out later."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Denial
reactions.example: "I'm fine. Stop making a big deal."
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay. I hear you. I'm still going to sit with you for a bit. You don't have to talk."
reactions.avoid: Walking away "to give space" when you're actually scared.

reactions.label: Crying / breakdown
reactions.example: They cry hard or shake.
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm here. You don't need to explain yet. Let's breathe together. We'll call someone who can help when you're ready."
reactions.avoid: Problem-solving mid-tears.

reactions.label: Admission + "don't tell anyone"
reactions.example: "I do it but please don't tell Dad."
reactions.parentResponse: "I won't share details. But this is bigger than just us. A doctor needs to help â they've seen this a thousand times."
reactions.avoid: Promising total secrecy.

reactions.label: Cold / flat affect
reactions.example: No emotion, short answers, detached.
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm going to make an appointment for us this week. You don't have to talk to me, but you need to talk to someone."
reactions.avoid: Mistaking flatness for "being okay".

reactions.label: Suicidal hints
reactions.example: "It would be easier if I wasn't here."
reactions.parentResponse: "Thank you for trusting me with that. I love you. Right now we're going together to call the crisis line / go to ER." STAY WITH THEM.
reactions.avoid: Treating it as a "teen phase" â treat every mention as real.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: self-harm may serve sensory regulation, not depression — requires specialist assessment. ADHD: impulsivity increases risk — removing means is priority #1.

redFlags.flag: Long sleeves or bandaging in hot weather.
redFlags.action: Same-day conversation and psychologist visit.

redFlags.flag: Rapid weight change in either direction.
redFlags.action: Check with a family doctor.

redFlags.flag: Giving away prized possessions.
redFlags.action: Classic pre-suicidal sign — requires immediate psychiatric assessment.

redFlags.flag: Searches for "painless methods" or specific mention of ending their life.
redFlags.action: Immediately call emergency services or the crisis line.

redFlags.flag: Ritualized food tracking or hiding food.
redFlags.action: Requires psychiatric assessment (eating disorder).

followUp: Remove means at home (razors, medication stockpiles, firearms locked).
Same-week appointment with a clinician experienced in adolescents.
Local crisis line saved in their phone AND yours.
Parent self-care: get your own therapist. This is heavy to carry alone.
en.repairAfterConflict: After a hard self-harm conversation: DO NOT leave the child alone for the next 24h. Same room — cooking nearby, reading. Bodily presence > words.
en.positiveReinforcement: Every disclosure of emotional pain — even clumsy — reward with: 'Thank you for telling me. That took courage.' Nothing more at this stage.
en.culturalContext: Crisis lines: 988 (US), Childline 0800 1111 (UK). Text HOME to 741741 (Crisis Text Line US/UK/CA). Find a private clinician TODAY if public waiting lists are long.

en.label: Violence & Gore
en.why: Repeated exposure to graphic real violence desensitizes kids and is linked to intrusive thoughts, anxiety, and sleep issues.
en.opening: "I noticed a blocked site with fairly graphic content with pretty graphic stuff. I'm not angry. Sometimes people watch that out of curiosity. How did it make you feel if you saw it?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: Check whether you're exposing the child to graphic news or adult conversations. 'Overheard' violence also traumatises. Turn off TV during war/accident talk.
pitfalls: Don't confuse fictional violence (games, horror) with real-world gore sites â different effects.
Don't overreact to one-time curiosity. Do react to patterns.

ageNotes.note: Focus on "real vs. pretend". Young brains don't reliably separate them.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: This is a peak age for "shock video" trading between friends. Ask who sent it.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Discuss desensitization honestly. "The 10th time is not the same as the 1st â your brain stops reacting."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Discuss desensitization honestly. "The 10th time is not the same as the 1st â your brain stops reacting."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "It was just a meme"
reactions.example: They frame it as a joke / edgy humor.
reactions.parentResponse: "Meme culture pushes this stuff hard. That doesn't make it harmless. How did it actually feel watching it?"
reactions.avoid: Dismissing the humor aspect as fake â it's real to them.

reactions.label: Bravado
reactions.example: "It was cool, didn't bother me."
reactions.parentResponse: "Maybe not now. Sometimes that kind of stuff shows up at 3 a.m. later. If that happens, come find me."
reactions.avoid: Lecturing about "you THINK you're tough".

reactions.label: Upset / disturbed
reactions.example: They admit it was horrible and they can't stop thinking about it.
reactions.parentResponse: "That makes sense. Images like that stick. Let's talk about what helps â sometimes it fades, sometimes a therapist is useful."
reactions.avoid: Minimizing ("it's just a video").

reactions.label: Dodging the topic
reactions.example: Topic switches every time you try.
reactions.parentResponse: Circle back once a day for a week in 60-second doses. "Just want to check â brain okay today?"
reactions.avoid: Giving up after one deflection.

reactions.label: Sharing / peer pressure
reactions.example: Friends send gore videos in group chats.
reactions.parentResponse: "You can mute or leave that chat. You don't owe them your reactions. Want help writing what to say?"
reactions.avoid: Confiscating the phone to "solve" it.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: sensory sensitivity can be extreme — one exposure = days of anxiety. ADHD: harder to resist the click — discuss pre-emptive blocking together.

redFlags.flag: Seeking out more extreme content over time.
redFlags.action: Talk about desensitisation.

redFlags.flag: Fascination with specific perpetrators or mass-violence events.
redFlags.action: Psychological consultation.

redFlags.flag: Threats of violence to self or others â immediate professional help.
redFlags.action: Immediate professional help.

redFlags.flag: Sleep disruption or nightmares lasting >2 weeks.
redFlags.action: Therapist experienced with teens.

followUp: Introduce a "news diet" â follow one credible source, drop gore/shock feeds.
Screen for anxiety/PTSD symptoms with a clinician if disturbance lingers.
Discuss how to block/report accounts in the apps they use.
en.repairAfterConflict: If nightmares follow — don't dismiss. Co-watch a 'neutraliser' — gentle nature film, comedy. The brain needs to overwrite.
en.positiveReinforcement: If the child closes a graphic video on their own and tells you — maturity. 'That was a hard choice. I'm proud of you.' Don't joke.
en.culturalContext: Many news sites show unfiltered graphic imagery. For kids under 12 — skip TV news. Use Common Sense Media for ratings; YouTube Kids with whitelist-only.

en.label: Dating Apps
en.why: Dating apps weren't built for minors. Predators, sextortion, and bodyshock content all hit hard.
en.opening: "A dating app was blocked — I'm curious, not accusing on your device. I'm curious â was it you, or did something auto-install? I just want to understand, not interrogate."
en.parentSelfRegulation: Your own dating history shapes what you project. Ask: 'Am I projecting fears?' Be ready for answers touching on orientation — a separate talk.
pitfalls: Don't assume it's sexual. It might be loneliness or wanting attention.
Don't make it about gender/orientation unless they bring it up â that conversation is separate.

ageNotes.note: Dating apps are explicitly for adults. Frame as a safety rule, not a shame rule.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Talk about catfishing and sextortion â these happen weekly in every country.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Discuss age-verification gaps. "These apps lie about being 18+. Adults target young accounts specifically."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Discuss age-verification gaps. "These apps lie about being 18+. Adults target young accounts specifically."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Embarrassment
reactions.example: Red-faced, wants the conversation over.
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay. One sentence then I'll drop it: adults on those apps target younger accounts on purpose. That's the only reason I care."
reactions.avoid: Dragging out the awkward.

reactions.label: "I'm just talking to friends"
reactions.example: They claim it's for socializing, not dating.
reactions.parentResponse: "I get it. Apps like Discord or Snap work for that without the adults-looking-for-teens problem."
reactions.avoid: Accusing them of lying without listening.

reactions.label: Revealing grooming attempt
reactions.example: "This older person asked me to send pictures."
reactions.parentResponse: "Thank you for telling me. You did nothing wrong. We're going to screenshot everything and report together â police/NCMEC/DyĹźurnet (PL)."
reactions.avoid: Panicking OUT LOUD â internal panic is fine.

reactions.label: Denial + device wipe
reactions.example: You later find the app was reinstalled + history cleared.
reactions.parentResponse: "I saw it again. I'd rather hear why than pretend. Let's figure out what need this is trying to fill."
reactions.avoid: One-off lecture without addressing the underlying loneliness.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: difficulty reading intent — may not realise online manipulation. Use concrete, literal examples. ADHD: fast attachment + impulsivity = elevated risk.

redFlags.flag: Secrecy around one specific contact.
redFlags.action: Same-day conversation — without blame.

redFlags.flag: New expensive gifts you didn't buy.
redFlags.action: Grooming signal — psychologist consultation.

redFlags.flag: Night-time texting with an unknown adult.
redFlags.action: App block + boundaries conversation.

redFlags.flag: Pressure to meet someone in person "just them".
redFlags.action: Immediate intervention and police report.

redFlags.flag: Any nude request or sent image â stop the conversation, document, report.
redFlags.action: Stop conversation, document, report to police and CyberTipline.

followUp: Enable MDM-level app restrictions, not just DNS.
Teach: screenshot + block + tell a trusted adult = always safe response to weird messages.
Know where to report: NCMEC (US), CEOP (UK), DyĹźurnet (PL), local police child-protection unit.
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child revealed grooming and you panicked — within 2 hours come back: 'I reacted with fear. I'm sorry. I'm ready to listen without interrupting now.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child shows you an online chat — EVEN if something looks off — start with 'Thank you for trusting me.' ONLY THEN assess.
en.culturalContext: Report to NCMEC CyberTipline (US 1-800-843-5678), CEOP (UK), or local child-protection police. Teens: Internet Matters, Common Sense Media.

en.label: Weapons
en.why: Curiosity about weapons is common in teens. The problem starts when "watching shooting videos" becomes "searching how to get one" or "how to build one".
en.opening: "I'd like to talk without panic — a page about weapons was blocked. I'm not assuming the worst â I want to know what caught your attention. A film, a game, something a friend said?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: If weapons are in the home (airgun counts) — LOCK before the talk, not during. Check your own search history for anything the child may have spotted.
pitfalls: Visiting these pages doesn't make a teenager a future attacker. Fascination with weapons is sometimes a normal phase.
Don't mock â shame pushes the fascination underground.
Don't bribe with "we'll buy you a shooting range membership" to distract. Talk directly.

ageNotes.note: Many cartoons and games show weapons as toys. Explain: "In real life, guns kill. We don't touch them, even if they look like toys."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: This is the age of first "how to disassemble a pistol" videos on YouTube. Ask if the idea came from friends â peer pressure is key.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Be honest: "Firearms in most countries require permits and psychological screening. Even airguns have age limits. Want to learn to shoot legally? Let's look at a range with an instructor."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Be honest: "Firearms in most countries require permits and psychological screening. Even airguns have age limits. Want to learn to shoot legally? Let's look at a range with an instructor."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "It's just a video / game"
reactions.example: They downplay â "I watched a pistol review in a game".
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay, I believe you. Show me exactly what â we'll watch together, see if it's a gaming channel or something else."
reactions.avoid: Rejecting their explanation without looking at the actual content.

reactions.label: Fascination with perpetrators
reactions.example: They ask about school shootings, know attackers' names.
reactions.parentResponse: "That worries me â not because I think you'd do it. Because I want to understand what pulls you toward it. Let's talk."
reactions.avoid: Silence or avoiding the topic. Fascination with perpetrators is a signal requiring conversation.

reactions.label: "I want to learn to shoot"
reactions.example: They directly say shooting sports interest them.
reactions.parentResponse: "That's a fair request. Let's find a shooting club that accepts minors with a parent. Better that than self-taught from the internet."
reactions.avoid: Automatic "no" without consideration â you'll push them to watch covertly.

reactions.label: Building weapons
reactions.example: They search "how to make a gun at home" or "ghost gun".
reactions.parentResponse: "Stop. This is felony-level stuff, not internet trivia. Sit down, we talk â what's making you search for this?"
reactions.avoid: Dismissing with "they won't actually do it". Treat it seriously.

reactions.label: Threats / joke-threats
reactions.example: "If I had a gun I'd wipe out half the class."
reactions.parentResponse: "I have to take what you said seriously. Even if you were joking. I love you â that's why we're going to talk to someone who can help us, right now."
reactions.avoid: Laughing it off, looking the other way. Treat every such statement as real.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: fascination is often mechanics (how it works) — not aggression. Distinguish. ADHD: impulsivity + access = risk even without intent.

redFlags.flag: Fascination with specific attacks, perpetrators, manifestos.
redFlags.action: Psychological consultation.

redFlags.flag: Searching "how to get a gun" or "how to build one".
redFlags.action: Immediate help — psychologist and possibly police.

redFlags.flag: Lists of people who "deserve it".
redFlags.action: Report to school and psychologist the same day.

redFlags.flag: Combination: social isolation + anger + weapons interest = immediate professional help.
redFlags.action: Red alert — combination requires immediate specialist intervention.

followUp: At home: secure every weapon (including airguns, bows, collector knives).
Book a psychologist visit â not as punishment, as "I want you to have someone to talk to about hard stuff".
Consider legal, supervised shooting sport if the interest is healthy.
Emergencies: 112 / 911 or local crisis hotline.
en.repairAfterConflict: After a failed conversation — don't 'fix' it the same day. Next day: 'I wonder what you wanted to tell me but couldn't.' Listen.
en.positiveReinforcement: When a child reports a peer threatening with a weapon — mature citizenship. 'You did the right thing. That was a hard choice.'
en.culturalContext: Firearm access laws vary by country. Interest in shooting sports: certified youth programmes (Boy Scouts shooting in US; supervised clubs from age 10 in EU).

en.label: Cyberbullying risk
en.why: Anonymous Q&A apps (NGL, Tellonym, Ask.fm) are designed so people write what they'd never say to your face. Teens read it personally â sometimes tragically.
en.opening: "One of your apps is on my mind — I want to know if everything is okay for you, you used an app where people write anonymously. Not to take it away â to know if anyone is hurting you there."
en.parentSelfRegulation: Your own school memories may flood you. If you were a victim — warn the child: 'I went through something like this. Your experience may be different.' Don't project.
pitfalls: Don't say "just turn it off". For a teen, that is their social world.
Don't read their messages without their knowledge â you lose trust.
Don't downplay â words online hurt as much as in the classroom, sometimes more because they're permanent.

ageNotes.note: Simple: "There are apps where people can write mean things and you don't know who wrote them. If anyone hurts you there, tell me right away."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: The hardest age. Ask directly: "Has anyone ever written something that made you not want to go to school?"
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Talk about how anonymous asks are often their own friend group, not strangers. Betrayal hurts more.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Talk about how anonymous asks are often their own friend group, not strangers. Betrayal hurts more.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Dismissal
reactions.example: "Nobody takes that stuff seriously."
reactions.parentResponse: "Maybe. Still, it's designed for you to read. Remember, I'm not checking your chats â can you tell me honestly how it actually lands with you?"
reactions.avoid: Insisting it hurts when they say it doesn't. Trust they can come back to this.

reactions.label: Admits bullying
reactions.example: "One anon has been saying I'm ugly for two weeks."
reactions.parentResponse: "Thank you for telling me. Let's save screenshots. Not to go after them now â to have proof if it escalates. Who do you suspect?"
reactions.avoid: Immediate "I'm going to the school" without their consent â they'll feel betrayed.

reactions.label: Self-blame
reactions.example: "Maybe they were right, something is wrong with me."
reactions.parentResponse: "Stop. This is a deliberate trick â they wrote something you're starting to believe. It's not the truth about you. I'll show you others who've been through this and why it's not your fault."
reactions.avoid: Dismissive "don't be silly" â that confirms to them the attack landed.

reactions.label: Revenge anger
reactions.example: "I'll find them and destroy them."
reactions.parentResponse: "I get it. It makes me angry too. But revenge spirals. Better plan: I'll show you how to report and block, and we'll think together about whether this is a school issue."
reactions.avoid: Encouraging revenge plans. The child is losing control; you keep it.

reactions.label: Withdrawal / silence
reactions.example: Not eating, skipping school, won't make eye contact.
reactions.parentResponse: "I can see something's off. I don't need to know today. But tomorrow we sit down. Meanwhile, phone stays next to you â promise you'll wake me if it gets hard."
reactions.avoid: Leaving the child alone with their silence for days.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: may not recognise teasing/bullying — show concrete examples, name them. ADHD: impulsive online replies escalate — teach the pause.

redFlags.flag: Sudden change in relationship with a specific friend group.
redFlags.action: Same-day conversation.

redFlags.flag: Avoiding school, stomach/headaches before leaving home.
redFlags.action: Conversation + school counsellor.

redFlags.flag: Deleting all photos from social media.
redFlags.action: A conversation about what changed in peer relationships.

redFlags.flag: Mentions of "not worth living" or "everyone hates me" â take seriously.
redFlags.action: Immediately call crisis line or ER.

followUp: Report and block the account/message in the app, together.
If it's school peers â decide to speak to the teacher together with your child, not behind their back.
Save a child helpline number (US: 988; UK: Childline 0800 1111; PL: 116 111) in their contacts.
Consider therapy â cyberbullying leaves marks similar to workplace mobbing.
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child shuts down after you urged a teacher conversation — respect for 24h. Then: 'I didn't mean to force you. We decide together, okay?'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child FIRST tells you about hate — biggest win. 'You dared to. That's what I'm most proud of.'
en.culturalContext: Crisis lines: Childline 0800 1111 (UK), 988 (US). StopBullying.gov; Bullying UK. School teachers have mandated reporting duties in most countries.

en.label: VPN & proxy
en.why: Trying to bypass filters is a signal, not a crime. Your kid is learning to think independently â the problem is a VPN also unlocks everything you were protecting them from.
en.opening: "I see you tried installing a VPN. I'm not angry â I'm curious what you wanted to watch or access. My guess: something I blocked?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: If you use a VPN at work — be honest: 'Yes, I use one too, but for different reasons.' A lie ('VPNs are evil') kills credibility in 10 seconds.
pitfalls: Don't pretend VPN is a hacker tool. You use it at work â be honest.
Don't escalate punishments â they'll install a better VPN next time.
Don't ignore it â one working VPN means the whole DNS block is bypassed.

ageNotes.note: Too young for technical depth. Enough: "This app gets around what we agreed at home about what we watch. Please don't install it without telling me."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Explain: "VPN is like switching TV channels through another country. Legal, but in our home we use the rules we agreed on together."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Talk as equals: "I know you know how it works. Instead of an arms race â tell me what you want unblocked, and we decide together."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Talk as equals: "I know you know how it works. Instead of an arms race â tell me what you want unblocked, and we decide together."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "For privacy"
reactions.example: They cite "right to privacy online".
reactions.parentResponse: "Privacy matters, agreed. But this is about bypassing rules we set together. Two different things. Let's start with what you actually want to see."
reactions.avoid: Lecturing "you don't have privacy yet". They do â but not freedom to break agreements.

reactions.label: "Everyone has a VPN"
reactions.example: They cite classmates.
reactions.parentResponse: "Possibly. Still, we agreed differently at home. Want to convince me to change the rule? Give me concrete reasons â I'm listening."
reactions.avoid: Going into "show me which ones".

reactions.label: Admits specific need
reactions.example: "I wanted to watch a show not available here."
reactions.parentResponse: "Thanks for honesty. Let's check if the show is legally available elsewhere, or agree on conditions under which I can say yes."
reactions.avoid: Refusal without conversation â you lose a chance at compromise.

reactions.label: Anger about control
reactions.example: "You don't trust me, you're spying on me!"
reactions.parentResponse: "I do trust you. My job isn't to trust blindly â it's to help you learn boundaries, so in a few years you set them yourself. Let's talk about how much oversight is okay for you."
reactions.avoid: Defensive "because I'm the parent".

reactions.label: Covert install
reactions.example: You find a VPN reinstalled after earlier conversations.
reactions.parentResponse: "I see you installed it again. This isn't about VPN anymore â it's about you lying. That hurts more than the app."
reactions.avoid: Punishment for VPN without addressing the broken agreement.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: bypassing blocks is often sport — redirect into legal IT security learning. ASD: holds rules tightly; breaking one usually has a concrete cause.

redFlags.flag: VPN used together with one specific messaging app â may indicate a hidden conversation.
redFlags.action: Conversation about who is part of that specific chat.

redFlags.flag: Regular browser history wiping.
redFlags.action: A conversation about what is being hidden.

redFlags.flag: Managing several email / Apple ID / Google accounts you didn't know about.
redFlags.action: A trust conversation — not about the app itself.

followUp: Enable Screen Time (iOS) or Family Link (Android) â block installing apps without approval.
Talk about one specific thing to unblock â negotiation instead of an arms race.
Check together why a given site was blocked â maybe unnecessarily.
en.repairAfterConflict: After a fight about a broken agreement: separate VPN from lying. 'I'm upset about the lying, not the VPN.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child says 'I want to install a VPN because X' — EXEMPLARY. 'Thank you for asking. Let's talk.' Pay them with attention.
en.culturalContext: VPNs are legal and popular. Set joint rules for specific apps and domains.

en.label: Dark web & Tor
en.why: In 99% of cases, dark-web interest is pop culture and curiosity. The other 1% is real risk â substances to buy, content you can't unsee.
en.opening: "Something I saw unsettled me — I want to understand what pulls you to Tor. Not making a scene â let's talk. What's the pull?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: Dark web triggers strong emotions (films, stereotypes). Research before the talk — credible documentary. If you are afraid, the child will sense it.
pitfalls: Don't turn it into a thriller â you'll give them the excitement of secrecy they crave.
Don't dismiss â "just curiosity" is sometimes a starting point.
Don't block without talking â the next step is learning how to bypass the block.

ageNotes.note: Skip the technical detail. Enough: "There are places online where people sell dangerous things, like drugs. Not for you."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Explain calmly: "Tor is a browser that hides who uses it. Not evil on its own â but 80% of what's there is criminal markets."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Respect their intelligence: "If online privacy truly interests you, there are safer paths. Let's watch a documentary together â then you decide."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Respect their intelligence: "If online privacy truly interests you, there are safer paths. Let's watch a documentary together â then you decide."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Cybersecurity interest
reactions.example: "I'm learning hacking / ethical hacking."
reactions.parentResponse: "Great topic. I know legal paths â CTFs, HackerOne courses, TryHackMe. Deal: no Tor, but a real course. Okay?"
reactions.avoid: Dismissing the passion â cybersecurity is a great career.

reactions.label: Curiosity after a show
reactions.example: "I watched a series, wanted to see what it looks like."
reactions.parentResponse: "Fine. I'll show you a documentary where a journalist describes what they found â without going live. Saves you images that stay for years."
reactions.avoid: Making it scarier ("you can't handle it"). A dare activates the teen brain.

reactions.label: Purchases
reactions.example: Clear signals they're looking for substances or illegal goods.
reactions.parentResponse: "Stop. This is a line we don't cross. You need to know: if you buy something and it comes out, you'll have a criminal case before 18. We talk now."
reactions.avoid: Accusations without data. If you suspect â ask directly, don't guess.

reactions.label: Showing friends
reactions.example: Uses Tor as a "cool trick" to impress friends.
reactions.parentResponse: "I get it â it looks cool. Problem: if any of you clicks a bad link, someone can see your address or show content you shouldn't see. Let's find a better way to impress."
reactions.avoid: Predicting the worst ("you'll be arrested"). Use concrete consequence instead.

reactions.label: Denial
reactions.example: "Wasn't me, must have been an update."
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm showing you exactly what I saw and when. Not accusing â asking for a conversation. Better to know than to fill in the blanks."
reactions.avoid: Accepting "not me" without a real conversation. If it truly wasn't them â it'll come back.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: interest in Tor may be genuine, passion-driven, without ill intent. Separate technical curiosity from risk. ADHD: fascination with the forbidden is impulsive — legal alternatives (CTFs).

redFlags.flag: Buying a crypto wallet alongside Tor use.
redFlags.action: Signals a planned purchase — needs conversation and possibly specialist.

redFlags.flag: Specific queries like "how to buy [substance]" or "how to get [item]".
redFlags.action: Immediate serious conversation + monitoring.

redFlags.flag: Isolation after a Tor session â may mean they saw something traumatic.
redFlags.action: Possible trauma exposure — psychologist visit.

redFlags.flag: Fascination with manifestos, "how-to" documents for dangerous things.
redFlags.action: Red alert — fascination with manifestos requires consultation.

followUp: Block installing new browsers (iOS Restrictions; Android Family Link).
Offer a legal privacy alternative: Brave, ProtonMail, Signal â if privacy is their goal.
Come back to the topic once a month for a quarter â it doesn't go away after one talk.
If they saw graphic content â a psychologist visit; dark-web imagery leaves a mark.
en.repairAfterConflict: If reaction was too harsh ('I want to punish you!'): within a day come back with an apology: 'I panicked. That was about me, not you.'
en.positiveReinforcement: Cybersecurity is a great career track. 'Good that you're curious. Check HackTheBox — same topic, legal.'
en.culturalContext: Legal CTF communities: HackTheBox, TryHackMe, PicoCTF. Local CERT sites publish scam notices. Cybersecurity scholarships often exist for teens.

en.label: Piracy
en.why: A show, a game, a textbook â "free from the internet" is a natural reflex. The problem: pirate sites are 90% malware, fake invoices, and data theft.
en.opening: "I noticed a blocked free-movie site and I want to talk about it. I'm not going to pretend no one does this. But let's talk about why it's not a great idea."
en.parentSelfRegulation: Be honest with yourself — did you torrent in your youth? Kids smell hypocrisy. If yes — admit: 'I did it too, that's how I know it's not simple.'
pitfalls: Don't moralize "you're stealing from artists". They'll point at Netflix's billions â and they're partly right.
Don't pretend you've never downloaded anything. Be honest about your own habits.
Don't lead with the legal argument â start with device safety.

ageNotes.note: Short rule: "Don't click on 'free' without asking. Those links often carry viruses."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Concrete: "Pirate sites make money from ads that install malware. Not paranoia â it's a business model."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Respectful: "I know Spotify or Netflix can feel expensive. Let's sit down â see what you actually watch, and decide what's worth paying for."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Respectful: "I know Spotify or Netflix can feel expensive. Let's sit down â see what you actually watch, and decide what's worth paying for."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Pragmatism
reactions.example: "I can't afford all these subscriptions."
reactions.parentResponse: "Fair. I'll show you the library â films, books, games, often free. And pick one subscription â it comes out of your allowance."
reactions.avoid: Dumping responsibility on the child ("no money â your problem").

reactions.label: "Everyone does it"
reactions.example: Lots of classmate arguments.
reactions.parentResponse: "Some probably do. I still don't want malware on our router. Let's pick one legal source and stick to it."
reactions.avoid: Discrediting their friends ("their parents don't care").

reactions.label: School textbooks
reactions.example: They pirate PDFs because "there's no other way".
reactions.parentResponse: "Common problem. Let's check the school's digital library, Open Library, Internet Archive. I'll show you how."
reactions.avoid: Pretending textbooks are cheap. They aren't â kids see this more clearly than we do.

reactions.label: Malware
reactions.example: Computer slowing down, pop-up ads, "my TikTok password changed".
reactions.parentResponse: "You have malware. Don't click any 'fixers from the internet'. We sit together â reboot, scan, change passwords. You're not the first."
reactions.avoid: Punishing for catching a virus. Better to go through the repair together.

reactions.label: Shame
reactions.example: They avoid the topic because "they know it's illegal".
reactions.parentResponse: "You're not a criminal. You're a teenager who wanted to watch a show. Thanks for honesty. Two things: check what got infected, and find a way to watch safely."
reactions.avoid: Using shame as leverage. Only deepens hiding.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: short jump 'I want → I click' — teach a 5-second pause. ASD: literal rule reading — explain 'this is illegal' concretely, with consequences.

redFlags.flag: Computer sending huge amounts of data at night (possible bot in your home network).
redFlags.action: Check device for malware.

redFlags.flag: Parents' card showing small, strange charges â card may be stolen.
redFlags.action: Freeze card, report to bank.

redFlags.flag: Child starts "cheap" selling of games/accounts in groups â may be trafficking stolen goods.
redFlags.action: Conversation + possible report to school.

followUp: Pick one legal subscription within budget together.
Install a free antivirus scanner and run a full scan.
Change passwords on important accounts â bank, email, Apple/Google IDs.
Suggest: public libraries, free-classics projects, legal YouTube channels.
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child is ashamed about a virus: avoid 'I told you so'. Focus on the fix: 'We repair together. You aren't the first or last.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child proposes 'I'll buy legally' — reward financially (match half) or verbally. Long-term.
en.culturalContext: Public libraries (Libby, Hoopla) — free and legal. Project Gutenberg for classics. Many schools offer digital-library access — show it.

en.label: High-risk crypto
en.why: "Buy this token, become a millionaire" videos are designed like ADHD in a bottle: short, hype, amplified by the algorithm. Teens lose savings faster than adults lose at gambling.
en.opening: "I'd like to talk — I noticed you checked crypto sites. I'm not forbidding it â I want to understand. Want to invest, earn, or just curious?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: If you invest in crypto — be honest about losses. Lying that 'it's all going great' teaches the child to lie too.
pitfalls: Don't pretend crypto doesn't exist. A classmate already "made money" and talks about it daily.
Don't lump everything together as a scam â there are legal exchanges, and there are meme tokens from TikTok ads. Not the same.
Don't say "if you lose, your problem". Losing the first âŹ100 at 14 builds a financial trauma that lasts years.

ageNotes.note: Unlikely for kids under 10 to watch crypto content. If so: "It's like playing with Monopoly money â except this money is real."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Show a real memecoin chart â pump and -95%. "This is what influencers present as a success."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Adult-to-adult: "Investing isn't gambling if you know the math. Let me show you compound interest, ETFs, and why pump.fun is a different league."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Adult-to-adult: "Investing isn't gambling if you know the math. Let me show you compound interest, ETFs, and why pump.fun is a different league."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "A friend made money"
reactions.example: Story about a classmate who "did a 10x".
reactions.parentResponse: "Maybe. Ask them how much they lost before, and how much they'll lose right after. People share wins, not balances."
reactions.avoid: Calling the friend a liar. They likely believe their own story.

reactions.label: Wants to start investing
reactions.example: "I have âŹ100 saved, I want to buy Solana."
reactions.parentResponse: "Okay, let's talk. 10% of that â we buy together, log it in a spreadsheet, watch for a year. The other 90% goes to a savings account â that's your safe base."
reactions.avoid: A flat "no". Better to teach in a controlled setting.

reactions.label: Lost money
reactions.example: Comes in ashamed â "I lost âŹ200".
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm sorry. That's an expensive but real economics lesson. Sit down â we'll unpack what happened so you know what to do differently. I'm not paying it for you, but we learn together."
reactions.avoid: Covering the loss silently. They'll learn "parents bail me out" instead of learning risk.

reactions.label: Pump & dump / meme coins
reactions.example: Uses sites like pump.fun, buys tokens from ads.
reactions.parentResponse: "I want you to know: that's where people like you are the product. Token creators sell you something that'll be worth zero in 3 days. Deal â show me any next buy before you click."
reactions.avoid: Total crypto ban. You can forbid â but you won't teach them to distinguish.

reactions.label: Scam
reactions.example: Someone online "helps" with investing, asks for a transfer to an unknown account.
reactions.parentResponse: "Stop. That's a scam, not an investment. Now: nothing more goes out, we block the person, report to the bank and to the police. You're not dumb â they are professional liars."
reactions.avoid: Yelling. The victim already feels ashamed; yelling deepens it.

en.neurodivergentNote: ADHD: dopamine from green candles is especially addictive — compulsion risk several times higher. ASD: passion for market maths — redirect to ETFs and compound interest.

redFlags.flag: Hours of trading-TikTok watching with racing heart / waking up at night to check.
redFlags.action: Conversation about algorithm and ad traps.

redFlags.flag: Selling belongings to fund "investments".
redFlags.action: Compulsion pattern — possible addiction specialist.

redFlags.flag: Taking a loan (from a friend, sibling, or an app) for crypto.
redFlags.action: Financial intervention + debt conversation.

redFlags.flag: Contact with an unknown person who "teaches trading".
redFlags.action: Classic scam pattern — report to bank and police.

followUp: Set a weekly cap on the amount they can move.
Open a savings account together with a visible interest rate â 5% at a bank, not "100x in a week".
Recommend realistic content: a local finance author, ETF basics.
If there's already a serious loss â do not hide it from the other parent; it's a family matter.
en.repairAfterConflict: After a fight about lost money: don't cut allowance 'as punishment'. That teaches hiding. 'You still have your next allowance. We'll pick what to do with it together.'
en.positiveReinforcement: If the child says 'I decided to wait' or 'I won't buy' — MATURITY. 'That's an adult-level decision. I see it.'
en.culturalContext: Financial regulators (SEC, FCA, KNF) publish scam warnings. Khan Academy finance module; Investopedia; The Motley Fool (in moderation).

en.label: Scam & phishing
en.why: "Free Robux, V-Bucks, Nitro" are the most popular bait for kids. They lose not only their own accounts, but also parents' saved cards.
en.opening: "I noticed a blocked 'free Robux' page got blocked. Not raising an alarm â I want you to know how it actually works. Okay?"
en.parentSelfRegulation: Show your own scam attempts (package SMS, bank SMS) as examples — include the child in 'guess which is a scam'. Joint analysis builds the habit.
pitfalls: Don't say "you're too smart to fall for it". These pages work because they target emotions, not logic.
Don't mock after the fact. Shame shuts down future reporting.
Don't ignore the signal â if they clicked once, they'll likely click again.

ageNotes.note: Simple: "No one online gives anything for free. If someone promises â they want something from you. Always show me before you click."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Be concrete: "See, this looks like a Microsoft page. But in the address bar it says r-n-i-c-r-o-s-o-f-t â small r and n, not m. That is how it looks."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Adult-to-adult: "Scammers don't go after the stupid â they go after the fast. Tired, rushed, emotional. Best rule: slow down before clicking."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Treat them as a young adult: ask what kind of help they want (if any). Adult-to-adult: "Scammers don't go after the stupid â they go after the fast. Tired, rushed, emotional. Best rule: slow down before clicking."
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: "I knew it was a scam"
reactions.example: Plays expert: "I was just checking what it looks like".
reactions.parentResponse: "Cool. Show me how you spotted it â I want to make sure you know the signs. The next bait will be better made."
reactions.avoid: Assuming they know automatically. A knowledge check beats a guess.

reactions.label: Clicked, embarrassed
reactions.example: Admits after â "I gave my login because they promised Robux".
reactions.parentResponse: "Thanks for telling me. Now, fast: change password, enable two-factor, check what's missing. Calm â happens to adults too."
reactions.avoid: The "I told you so" lecture. Priority is damage control, not venting.

reactions.label: Parent's card gone
reactions.example: In a game app, your card shows purchases you didn't make.
reactions.parentResponse: "You're not in trouble for trusting. You're in a conversation about how it happened and what we do next. I'm calling the bank, and we file the report together."
reactions.avoid: Blaming the child for the financial loss. The system had no block.

reactions.label: Denial
reactions.example: "I didn't click, the app did itself."
reactions.parentResponse: "I'm showing you something got installed. Not going into who's at fault â important that we fix it. Maybe it was a trick, maybe not â we'll check."
reactions.avoid: Interrogation until they confess. Fix first, talk after.

reactions.label: Friend got scammed
reactions.example: Tells you about a friend who lost an account or money.
reactions.parentResponse: "Oh no. Good you know. Ask if her parents already know â important to block the card if payments were involved. You can help her â here are concrete steps."
reactions.avoid: Brushing it off. This is a chance for your kid to learn to respond before they're next.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: click-on-everything isn't stupidity but trust in messages. Literally teach scam signatures. ADHD: hurry = click. Two-second pause before any link.

redFlags.flag: Strange SMS from "courier" / "bank" / "Netflix" with a link.
redFlags.action: Teach recognition together — by address and tone.

redFlags.flag: Kid searching for "free stuff online" â pattern, not a one-off.
redFlags.action: A conversation about emotional triggers.

redFlags.flag: Changed payment method in App Store / Google Play.
redFlags.action: Account audit, password change, platform contact.

redFlags.flag: Contact in game apps with strangers "giving crates" or "helping with trading".
redFlags.action: Report to platform and cybercrime unit.

followUp: Enable two-factor auth on every account â everywhere possible.
Remove saved cards from gaming/app-store accounts â every purchase asks for a password.
If bank details were shared / money transferred â report to the bank and to your national cybercrime unit.
Agree on a rule: "Never a link from an SMS â always type the site manually."
en.repairAfterConflict: If the child lost data or money — priority is protecting the account, not venting. After the fix: 'This isn't your fault. They're pros.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When the child RECOGNISES a scam — even a tiny one — notice it. 'Nice catch. A few years ago I would have fallen for that.'
en.culturalContext: Report to FTC (US), Action Fraud (UK), local police cybercrime units. Banks have 24/7 hotlines to freeze cards. Never follow a link from SMS.

en.label: Peer pressure
en.why: For kids aged 10–14, peer relationships are developmentally MORE important than the parent bond. This isn't pathology — it's a stage. But without conversation, a child picks whatever gives immediate group acceptance, even if they know it harms them.
en.opening: "I'd like to talk about something I remember from my own teens — how you sometimes did something dumb just so you weren't 'out'. I'm curious how this looks in your class."
en.parentSelfRegulation: Recall your own worst decision at age 13, made to impress. If you remember, it's easier to talk without moral high ground.
pitfalls: Don't say 'just don't listen to them' — developmentally impossible at this age.
Don't mock their friend group — they'll feel the attack on themselves.
Don't plan 'I'll call the other parents' without your child's consent.

ageNotes.note: Family still > group. Teach roles: 'A friend is someone who still likes you after you said no.'
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Concrete thinking (Piaget). The child cannot yet separate intent from action or understand sarcasm. Use literal language and concrete images.

ageNotes.note: Critical moment — the group starts to dominate. Talk through concrete situations ('What would you do if X pressured you into Y?'). Scenarios > bans.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Beginning of abstract thinking. The peer group grows in importance; a strong need for fairness and fitting in.

ageNotes.note: Peak pressure. Offer a 'parent alibi' — 'If you ever want to leave a party, text me an owl emoji 🦉, I'll pick you up and say it's my fault.' This saves lives.
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Erikson: identity vs. role confusion. The "imaginary audience" effect peaks — the teen feels everyone is watching and judging.

ageNotes.note: Autonomous decisions grow, but impulsivity is still high. Talk as with an adult: 'How do you tell a friend from someone who uses you?'
ageNotes.developmentalContext: Dopaminergic reward pathways still maturing. Emotional over logical decision-making; they CAN reason but emotion leads. Treat as a young adult, but keep a safety net.

reactions.label: Dismissal
reactions.example: 'Nobody makes me do anything here.'
reactions.parentResponse: 'Okay. Have you recently done something you wouldn\'t have done if they weren\'t there? Even a small thing.'
reactions.avoid: Insisting there definitely is pressure.

reactions.label: Anger / defending the group
reactions.example: 'They\'re my friends, don\'t badmouth them!'
reactions.parentResponse: 'I\'m not badmouthing. I\'m asking about YOU in that group — how YOU feel there.'
reactions.avoid: Attacking specific people.

reactions.label: Revealing the situation
reactions.example: 'Tom told me to do X so I\'d stay in the group.'
reactions.parentResponse: 'Thank you for telling me. I\'ll help you find a way out — but first: how is this for you right now?'
reactions.avoid: Jumping to intervention planning.

reactions.label: Tired of pressure
reactions.example: 'I don\'t want to hang out with them anymore.'
reactions.parentResponse: 'That\'s important. You don\'t have to explain. I can help you plan the next days — or I can just be around.'
reactions.avoid: 'I told you so.'

reactions.label: Wants to drop an old friend
reactions.example: 'I have to stop talking to Kate, the rest don\'t like her.'
reactions.parentResponse: 'Hard choice. Think about what you\'d say to her in 5 years. I won\'t choose for you — but don\'t drop the question.'
reactions.avoid: Forcing 'you must be loyal'.

en.neurodivergentNote: ASD: the child may not recognise being used — teach concrete signatures ('if THEY always initiate and YOU never — that's not friendship'). ADHD: quickly caught up in 'coolness' — help them pause before agreeing.

redFlags.flag: Suddenly cutting off old friends.
redFlags.action: Conversation about what changed.

redFlags.flag: New friend group + sudden style shift (clothes, slang, values).
redFlags.action: Shared meal with the new group — not a trap, a chance to meet.

redFlags.flag: Unexplained cash, cigarettes, alcohol.
redFlags.action: Direct conversation + school contact.

redFlags.flag: Group threats if the child refuses.
redFlags.action: Document and report to teacher / school counsellor.

redFlags.flag: Child does things they're ashamed of — and still returns to the group.
redFlags.action: Individual therapy — self-esteem work.

followUp: Agree on a 'rescue code' — an emoji or phrase after which the parent picks them up, no questions.
Suggest an alternative group — sports team, club, scouts — where identity is built on something other than 'being cool'.
For 2 weeks, 5 minutes a day: 'How were people with you today?' — no judgement.
If pressure lasts > a month and behaviour changes — school counsellor or private psychologist.
en.repairAfterConflict: If you attacked their group and the door slammed — write: 'I handled this badly. Your friends aren't my business, but YOU are. We'll come back to this when you want.'
en.positiveReinforcement: When a child says 'I said no today because I didn't want to' — that's a moment. Not 'see, it worked'. Rather: 'That must have been hard. How are you with it now?'
en.culturalContext: School counsellors in most countries have mandated reporting duties for psychological pressure and bullying. If the school minimises — written complaint to the board. Resources: StopBullying.gov, Anti-Bullying Alliance (UK).

changes: Split ageNotes into 4 concrete ranges (6–9, 10–12, 13–15, 16–18) with developmentalContext.
Converted redFlags to structured objects with severity, action and referToSpecialist.
Rewrote opening lines as I-messages (avoiding accusatory "I saw you…").
Added parentSelfRegulation, neurodivergentNote, repairAfterConflict, positiveReinforcement, culturalContext per scenario.
Localised EN follow-up resources with country-specific helplines.
